content:
I need to find a way to store a CStr into an array. I found a way to do it at runtime but I would like to do it at compile time since that's what all the drivers seems to do on the C side, like here.

For reference here is the corresponding C strcut:

struct spi_device_id {
    char name[SPI_NAME_SIZE];
    kernel_ulong_t driver_data; /* Data private to the driver */
};

My idea was to create a const fn new(cstr: &CStr, ...) to be able to put the array of SpiDeviceId in a static as you would have done in C. Is there a way to output as string as an array directly? I think you can do it with b"..." for the str type but this is an utf-8 string not a CStr

Is there a way to output as string as an array directly

I couldn't parse this, what exactly do you need? For a fixed size array you could just fill it byte per byte.

Sorry, english is not my native tongue.
I need to do CStr to fixed size array, but at compiles time

you could just fill it byte per byte.

That's what I want to. The user give me a CStr (created with the c_str! macro) and inside the const fn new(...) I would write the bytes in the array of the struct.
Yeah I'm using that for the current version. What I'm missing is filling the array with the bytes but at compile time
You can just use a while loop.
Oh! This is compile time now ? Thanks!
Yup that's working great, thanks you very much :)
